content.text: Now I understand the structure. A2 has a date (January 29, 2016), and B5 needs a SUMPRODUCT formula that sums all paychecks (B+C) from 'Pay Dates' where the month matches the month of A2.

input.command:
python3 -c "
import openpyxl
wb = openpyxl.load_workbook('input.xlsx')
ws = wb['Overview']

# SUMPRODUCT to sum all paychecks (B+C) from Pay Dates where month matches A2's month
formula = '=SUMPRODUCT((MONTH(\'Pay Dates\'!A2:A27)=MONTH(A2))*(\'Pay Dates\'!B2:B27+\'Pay Dates\'!C2:C27))'
ws['B5'] = formula
ws['B5'].number_format = '\"$\"#,##0.00'

wb.save('output.xlsx')
print('Done. B5 =', formula)
"
input.description: Write SUMPRODUCT formula to B5 and save

content.text:
Saved `output.xlsx` with the formula in `Overview!B5`:

```
=SUMPRODUCT((MONTH('Pay Dates'!A2:A27)=MONTH(A2))*('Pay Dates'!B2:B27+'Pay Dates'!C2:C27))
```

This uses `MONTH()` to extract the month from both A2 (the selected date) and each date in the Pay Dates sheet, then sums all matching paychecks (columns B and C). So if January 29 is selected in A2, B5 will total all January paychecks: 800+500+900+600+1000+1000 = **$4,800.00**.

result:
Saved `output.xlsx` with the formula in `Overview!B5`:

```
=SUMPRODUCT((MONTH('Pay Dates'!A2:A27)=MONTH(A2))*('Pay Dates'!B2:B27+'Pay Dates'!C2:C27))
```

This uses `MONTH()` to extract the month from both A2 (the selected date) and each date in the Pay Dates sheet, then sums all matching paychecks (columns B and C). So if January 29 is selected in A2, B5 will total all January paychecks: 800+500+900+600+1000+1000 = **$4,800.00**.
